name: Smallmouth bass

tactics.notes: Rocky points, gravel bars, current seams.
Streamers in current, poppers in calm water.

notes: Cool-water predator. In rivers, target current breaks and rocky structure.

name: Largemouth bass

tactics.notes: Weedlines, lily pads, submerged timber.

notes: Ambush predator — fish cover and structure. Slow presentations in cold water, faster in warm.

name: Spotted bass

seasons.label: Pre-spawn through fall, deeper than largemouth

tactics.notes: Steeper banks and offshore structure than largemouth.
Deep water in summer — 25–40 ft.

notes: AKA Kentucky bass. Schoolier than largemouth, holds deeper in summer reservoirs.

name: Shoal bass

tactics.notes: Holding behind boulders in shoal current.
Wade rocky shoals; cast tight to boulder seams.

notes: Native to Chattahoochee, Flint, and Apalachicola drainages. Fight pound-for-pound harder than smallmouth.

name: Redeye / Coosa bass

tactics.notes: Top-water explosions on small mountain streams.
Small streams, clear water, sight casting.

notes: Native Coosa drainage species — colorful, rarely over 12 in. AL state fish.

name: Butterfly peacock

tactics.notes: Sight-fish bedding pairs in spring. Burn lures across the surface.

notes: South FL canals and lakes only — Miami area. Introduced to control tilapia; spectacular fighters that destroy topwaters.

name: Walleye

tactics.notes: Troll along contour breaks at 12–25 ft.
Vertical jig over structure at dusk.

notes: Low-light feeder — dawn, dusk, and overcast days are best.

name: Sauger

tactics.notes: Vertical jig in tailwater eddies, 15–30 ft.

notes: Below TN River dams (Pickwick, Wilson, Wheeler) in winter. Smaller, darker cousin of walleye — schoolier, more current-tolerant.

name: Hybrid striped bass / wiper

tactics.notes: Run a topwater through surface jumps; otherwise troll deep flats.

notes: Sterile cross — stocked widely in mid-South reservoirs. Brutal fighters; school-feed on shad.

name: White bass

tactics.notes: Stack up in current below dams during spawn — limit out in 30 minutes.

notes: Spring run is the most reliable freshwater bite of the year. Kids love them — non-stop action.

name: Yellow perch

tactics.notes: Vertical fish over weed edges in 15–25 ft.

notes: Schooling — when you find one, you find a hundred. Saginaw Bay and Lake Erie are gold standards.

name: Rock bass

tactics.notes: Rocks, snags, bridge pilings.
Cast tight to cover; bite is aggressive.

notes: Bycatch when smallmouth fishing — but a fun panfish in their own right. Bug-eyed red 'goggle eyes'.

name: Northern pike

seasons.label: Open water; ice fishing also productive

tactics.notes: Steel leader is required — they bite through fluoro.

notes: Weed edges and cabbage beds. Aggressive ambush predator.

name: Muskellunge (musky)

tactics.notes: Heavy gear, fluorocarbon leader. Figure-8 at boatside on every cast.

notes: The fish of 10,000 casts. Lake St. Clair and Cave Run are major fisheries.

name: Tiger muskie

tactics.notes: Faster cadence than pure muskie. Steel leader.

notes: Sterile pike × muskie hybrid. Stocked in many southern reservoirs. Aggressive — easier to hook than pure muskie.

name: Lake trout

seasons.label: Year-round — surface Jan-May + Oct-Dec; downriggers 60-130 ft Jun-Sep

lures: Cowbells with sucker meat or Spin-N-Glo
tactics.notes: 60–120 ft on downriggers in summer; surface trolling in spring.
Drop to bottom over humps.

notes: Deep, cold structure. Summer fish push to 80–150 ft on the Great Lakes.

name: Splake

tactics.notes: Through the ice, 20–50 ft over rocks.
Surface troll in spring.

notes: Brook × lake trout hybrid. Northern MI inland lakes — stocked, sterile.

name: Brook trout

seasons.label: Open water; high-elevation streams stay cool all summer

tactics.notes: Short, accurate casts to pocket water in tight cover.
Where legal — many brookie streams are fly-only.

notes: Southern Appalachian native (Smokies, Blue Ridge); MI UP and high-elevation lakes. Small, colorful, eager.

name: Brown trout

seasons.label: Open water — peak Hex hatch June

tactics.notes: Streamers for size, dries during a hatch.

notes: Even when nothing is hatching, big browns eat sculpins, crayfish, and other trout. Strip streamers along cut banks.

name: Rainbow trout

tactics.notes: Tailwater rainbows love midges and small mayflies.

notes: Caney Fork and South Holston rainbows feed on midges all winter.

name: Steelhead

seasons.label: Great Lakes troll summer; fall run Oct-Dec; spring drop-back Mar-May; winter holdovers Jan-Feb

tactics.notes: High-stick nymphing, indicator, or swung streamer.
Float-fishing with beads is the gold standard in MI.

notes: Manistee, Pere Marquette, St. Joseph (MI + IN). Trail Creek (IN) is a top spot. Tributaries to Lake Michigan / Lake Huron.

name: King (Chinook) salmon

seasons.label: Great Lakes troll Apr–Aug; staging Aug; river runs Sep–Oct; lingering until ice-up

tactics.notes: Great Lakes summer trolling — 60–120 ft.
River runs — drift through holding water.

notes: Manistee, Pere Marquette, Big Manistee see major fall runs. Float fishing with skein is highly productive.

name: Coho (silver) salmon

seasons.label: Top-of-column Great Lakes troll Apr–Aug; river run Sep–Oct

tactics.notes: Top 30 ft of the Great Lakes water column in summer.
Swing for fresh, chrome fish on river runs.

notes: More aggressive than kings on a hook; smaller (8–15 lb) but faster runs.

name: Pink salmon

seasons.label: Odd-year-only run on Lake Superior tribs

tactics.notes: Small fish (2–4 lb), light tackle in shallow tribs.

notes: Lake Superior tributaries. Odd-numbered years only. Two-year lifecycle — most fish are 2 lb.

name: Atlantic salmon

seasons.label: Great Lakes troll Apr–Aug; St. Marys + Boyne River runs Jul–Sep

tactics.notes: St. Marys is a classic float trip.

notes: Limited Great Lakes stocking — St. Marys River, Boyne River. Different fight than Pacific salmon.

name: Lake whitefish

tactics.notes: Ice fishing in 40–80 ft on Saginaw / Grand Traverse bays.

notes: Northern MI ice-fishing favorite. Delicate fight, excellent table fare smoked.

name: Burbot (eelpout)

tactics.notes: Set tip-ups at dusk, jig 80–150 ft straight down.

notes: Only native freshwater cod. Ugly, snaky, but firm white meat — 'poor man's lobster'.

name: Lake sturgeon

seasons.label: Open-water — check state regs for narrow season

tactics.notes: Heavy gear, no leader needed. Slow bite, hard fight.

notes: Protected — catch & release in most waters; tight quota on Black Lake / Otsego (MI). Living dinosaurs, can top 80 in.

name: Rainbow smelt

lures: Dip net at night during the run
tactics.notes: Tribs of Lake Superior + Lake Michigan during the late-April run.

notes: Mostly a dip-net fishery during the spring run. Battered + fried, head and all.

name: Common carp

tactics.notes: Sight-cast tailing fish on flats; lead the fish, don't strip.

lures: Sweet corn / dough bait on a bottom rig
tactics.notes: Hair rig + method ball is the European standard.

notes: Underrated freshwater quarry — strong fight, demands stealth on the flats. The 'freshwater bonefish'.

name: Channel catfish

seasons.label: Most active May–Sep, esp. after rain

tactics.notes: Bottom-fish holes, current seams, eddies after a fresh rain.

notes: The classic farm-pond fish. Big channels move into shallow water at night.

name: Blue catfish

tactics.notes: Anchor on deep ledges or drift with the wind in big reservoirs.

notes: TN River chain (Wheeler, Wilson, Pickwick) holds world-record class fish. Tail-water below dams in winter.

name: Flathead catfish

tactics.notes: Anchor near log jams and current seams; let live bait swim.

notes: Lives in cover — log jams, undercut banks. Eats only live prey. 50+ lb common.

name: Yellow bullhead

tactics.notes: Slow current, mud-bottom holes.

notes: Smaller than channel cats but mash-the-rod aggressive. Good first fish for kids.

name: Freshwater drum (sheepshead)

tactics.notes: Bottom-feeders — drag soft plastics on rocky bottom.

notes: Often bycatch for walleye and bass; strong fighters that grunt audibly. (Not to be confused with saltwater sheepshead.)

name: Bluegill / panfish

tactics.notes: Cast a popper at sundown over a weed line — non-stop action.
Slow it down on the spawn beds.

notes: Great for kids and for warming up. Spawn beds are obvious in shallow water in June.

name: Redear sunfish (shellcracker)

seasons.label: Spawn beds May–June; larger than bluegill

lures: Red worm on small hook + split shot
tactics.notes: Bottom over sandy shoreline; they eat snails.

notes: Reach 2+ lb in southern lakes. AKA 'shellcracker' — they crack snail shells with pharyngeal teeth.

name: Crappie

tactics.notes: Vertical drop over brush piles.

notes: Suspended around timber and brush. Spider rigging works on big reservoirs.

name: Striped bass (freshwater)

tactics.notes: Cherokee, Tims Ford, Norris — troll deep water in summer.
Surface feeds at first/last light in fall.

notes: TN reservoirs hold massive stripers; cool tailwater outflows hold them through summer.

name: Longnose gar

lures: Nylon rope fly (no hook — teeth tangle)
tactics.notes: Sight-cast to basking fish; their bony jaws make hookup ratio tough.

notes: Prehistoric — armor scales, alligator-like snout. Bowfishing is a common alternative.

name: Alligator gar

seasons.label: Hot summer in deep river holes

tactics.notes: Heavy gear (50+ lb braid). Hook in the corner of the jaw — bony mouth needs time.

notes: Apex freshwater predator — can exceed 7 ft. TN River chain and Mobile delta hold the southern population.

name: Bowfin (dogfish)

tactics.notes: Slow, stinky, weedy water.

notes: Tough fighters, ugly and prehistoric. Hand-of-the-bowfin caution — they bite hard.

name: Bullseye snakehead

tactics.notes: Throw topwater over surfacing fry pods — they crush the bait.

notes: Invasive — kill what you catch (FL). Air-breathing apex predator in Broward / Palm Beach canals.

name: Oscar

tactics.notes: Visible fish in clear south-FL canals — sight-cast.

notes: Invasive cichlid, established south of Lake Okeechobee. Hard-fighting; some anglers love them.

name: Mayan cichlid

tactics.notes: Cast tight to mangrove roots and undercut banks.

notes: South FL invasive — Everglades canals, brackish marshes. Spawning pairs are very aggressive.

name: Redfish (red drum)

tactics.notes: Sight-cast tailing fish on the low tide flats.
Work potholes and oyster bars on the falling tide.

notes: Time the falling tide on grass flats. The wake of a redfish in 8 inches of water is its own reason to fish.

name: Snook

seasons.label: Spring and summer in the passes; fall in backcountry

tactics.notes: Ambush points — mangrove roots, dock lights, passes.
Strip fast along mangrove edges.

notes: Closed season Dec–Mar (gulf) — check FWC rules before keeping any.

name: Spotted sea trout

tactics.notes: Grass flats, 3–5 ft of water, on a moving tide.

notes: Schoolies hit anything; gators (24+ in) are loners around structure.

name: Tarpon

tactics.notes: Lead the fish, strip-set, hold on.

lures: Live crab on the bridge tide
tactics.notes: Bridges, channels, passes.

notes: Catch-and-release only in FL. The fight of a lifetime.

name: Bonefish

seasons.label: Best Mar–Nov; cold fronts push them off

tactics.notes: Wade or pole the flats; lead tailing fish by 4–6 ft.

notes: Keys and Biscayne Bay. Catch-and-release tradition; the original 'gray ghost'.

name: Permit

tactics.notes: Cast a foot in front of tailing fish; long, slow strips.
Drift live crab past a wreck or flat.

notes: The hardest of the flats grand slam (tarpon + bonefish + permit). Picky and spooky.

name: Southern flounder

lures: Live mud minnow on jig head
tactics.notes: Slow drag along sandy bottom near structure.

notes: Inlets and channels in the fall — they stack up before moving offshore to spawn.

name: Sheepshead (saltwater)

seasons.label: Year-round inshore; biggest schools Feb–Apr pre-spawn

tactics.notes: Drop straight down on bridge pilings, dock posts, jetty rocks.

notes: Convict-striped, human-teeth nibblers. 'Set the hook before they bite' — they pick the bait clean.

name: Black drum

seasons.label: Spring spawn — big bull drum near passes

lures: Half blue crab on circle hook
tactics.notes: Slow bottom-bite around passes and bridges.

notes: Big drum (40–80 lb) are mostly catch-and-release (worm in flesh); slot fish are excellent table fare.

name: Tripletail

tactics.notes: Sight-cast to fish hanging under buoys, crab traps, weed lines.
Pitch past the structure, let it drift back.

notes: Lazy-looking but explosive on the hook. Best table fish in the Gulf — many anglers say.

name: Pompano

seasons.label: Fall through spring in the surf

tactics.notes: Surf trough just past the first wave break.

notes: Surf-fishing favorite. Sand fleas are the gold-standard bait — sift them at the wave line.

name: Spanish mackerel

tactics.notes: Look for diving terns and run-and-gun. Wire leader.

notes: Fast, schooling — they're under the birds. Excellent fresh, lousy frozen.

name: King mackerel

seasons.label: Spring + fall migrations along the coast

lures: Slow-trolled live cigar minnow on stinger rig
tactics.notes: Slow troll 2–4 kts around bait pods near reefs.

notes: Trophy 'smoker kings' top 50 lb. Wire stinger rig is mandatory — they cut mono.

name: Cobia

seasons.label: Spring migration along the Gulf and Atlantic coasts

tactics.notes: Sight-cast to fish following rays, turtles, or under buoys.

notes: Spring run is THE event on the Panhandle. Strong, dirty fighters — they don't quit.

name: Jack crevalle

seasons.label: Warm months in inshore + nearshore waters

tactics.notes: Crashing schools — burn topwater across the surface, hold on.

notes: Pound-for-pound brutes. Trash on the dinner plate, but they save slow days.

name: Ladyfish (poor man's tarpon)

tactics.notes: Fast retrieve along beach trough or inlet.
Multi-jump fights, often in schools.

notes: Slimy and skinny, but high-flying acrobatic fighters. Great kid-and-beginner fish in the surf.

name: Bluefish

seasons.label: Spring + fall migrations through the Carolinas

tactics.notes: Surf and inlet blitzes — birds give them away.

notes: Sharp teeth — wire leader recommended. Bleed and ice immediately; oily meat goes off fast.

name: False albacore (little tunny)

tactics.notes: Tight clousers in chartreuse + white work too. Cast leading the fish; long strips.
Quick retrieve through busting fish.

notes: The Cape Lookout fall albie run is bucket-list. Aggressive, sight-feeding tuna under the birds.

name: Whiting (sea kingfish)

tactics.notes: Surf trough on light tackle.

notes: Sweet-tasting surf fish. Often confused with king mackerel but unrelated.

name: Spot

lures: Bloodworm on small hook + bottom rig
tactics.notes: Pier or surf, bottom-rig in 5–15 ft.

notes: Hand-sized, tasty, easy. Pier fishing tradition in NC — limits in an hour during the run.

name: Atlantic croaker

tactics.notes: Bays, sounds, surf — bottom rig with small hooks.

notes: Croaks audibly when landed. Workhorse panfish of the southeast inshore.

name: Mangrove (gray) snapper

tactics.notes: Mangrove roots, dock lights, jetty rocks. Light fluoro leader — they're spooky.

notes: FL Keys + mainland resident snapper. Spawn aggregations in summer hold the biggest fish offshore.

name: Yellowtail snapper

tactics.notes: Anchor over reef, chum + drift back light leader.

notes: Keys + Atlantic reef. Schools just off the bottom — chum bag is the secret.

name: Red snapper

seasons.label: Federal season opens early summer — verify each year

lures: Live cigar minnow on circle hook
tactics.notes: Drop straight to wrecks and reefs in 80–250 ft.

notes: Heavily regulated — short federal season, check NOAA before keeping. AL has the largest reef pyramid in the Gulf.

name: Gag grouper

lures: Live pinfish on heavy jig head
tactics.notes: Drop to reefs or ledges. Lock the drag — winch them off the structure.

notes: Inshore grouper option in 60–120 ft. Crash dives back into the rocks if you give them line.

name: Red grouper

tactics.notes: Live bottom in 80–150 ft. Less structure-tight than gag.

notes: Stays a bit shallower than gag and fights less violently. Excellent table grouper.

name: Greater amberjack

lures: Live blue runner on circle hook
tactics.notes: Drop to wrecks 100–250 ft. Crank fast on the jig.

notes: Reef donkeys — strong, dirty fighters. Verify season + size limit before keeping.

name: Gray triggerfish

lures: Cut squid on small circle hook
tactics.notes: Tiny circle hook — triggerfish mouths are small. Anchor on reef.

notes: Excellent table fare; gulf reefs in 80–150 ft. Small bites, careful sets.

name: Hogfish

seasons.label: Open summer in the gulf; year-round Atlantic

tactics.notes: Slow current over patch reefs in 30–80 ft.

notes: Best-eating fish in the gulf, full stop. Picky eaters — they prefer real shrimp.

name: Mahi-mahi (dolphin)

tactics.notes: Troll the weed lines and floating debris offshore.

notes: Find a weed line in 600+ ft and run it. Run-and-gun on sea-surface-temp breaks.

name: Wahoo

lures: Heavy bullet head + ballyhoo at 12–18 kts
tactics.notes: High-speed troll — single-strand wire leader.

notes: 30-mph torpedo. Cape Lookout NC + south FL deep-drop ledges hold them. Bring a wire rig.

name: Blackfin tuna

tactics.notes: Often mixed with mahi on the weed lines and bait pods.

lures: Vertical knife jig at first light
tactics.notes: Mark fish on sonar, drop straight down.

notes: Small tuna (10–30 lb) but world-class table fare. Excellent on light tackle.

name: Atlantic sailfish

seasons.label: Cold-front-driven runs Dec–Mar on Atlantic coast

tactics.notes: Slow troll edge of the Gulf Stream. Drop-back on bite — let them eat.

notes: Sailfish capital is Stuart FL in winter. Catch-and-release; light tackle billfishing's peak experience.

name: Goliath grouper

lures: Whole bonita or jack on heavy circle
tactics.notes: Heavy gear (130+ lb braid). Hand-to-hand combat off wrecks.

notes: Catch & release only. 100–400 lb common, 600+ lb possible. Wrecks off SW FL coast.

name: Atlantic striped bass

tactics.notes: Surf or jetties; cast through bait blitzes.

notes: OBX winter run blitz fishery — when it's on, it's epic. Slot/size regs strict.

name: Blue tilapia

tactics.notes: Sight-cast to bedding fish in spring.
Bowfishing is the common alternative.

notes: Invasive but established. Excellent eating, easy to catch off beds.

name: Clown knifefish

tactics.notes: Dawn / dusk along canal edges.

notes: Aquarium escapee, established in south FL. Big — 10+ lb — and unusual table fare.

name: Skipjack herring

tactics.notes: Tailwaters below TN River dams; cast small flash in current seams.

notes: Mostly fished as cut bait for blue cats and stripers — but a fun light-tackle bonus species.

name: Suwannee bass

seasons.label: Spring through fall in clear-water rivers

tactics.notes: Tight to rocky shoals and limestone ledges in clear rivers.
Sight-fish in clear current.

notes: Native to Suwannee + Santa Fe + Ochlockonee drainages. Small (max ~3 lb) but pugnacious in cold spring-fed rivers.

name: Alabama bass

seasons.label: Pre-spawn through fall, deeper in summer

tactics.notes: Steep points and offshore humps; schoolier than largemouth.
Deep summer water 25–45 ft.

notes: Recognized species since 2008. Invasive in some NC + GA reservoirs where it outcompetes natives.

name: Pumpkinseed

tactics.notes: Shallow vegetated bays, drop a worm and watch the float.
Cast tight to lily pad edges.

notes: Bright orange-and-blue-cheeked panfish of cool, clear MI lakes. Closely tied with bluegill in mixed populations.

name: Green sunfish

tactics.notes: Crashes any small lure thrown near cover. Cattle ponds, ditches, anywhere wet.

notes: Aggressive, tolerates poor water — survives where everything else dies. Often the only fish in farm ponds.

name: Longear sunfish

tactics.notes: Sight-fish on gravel runs.
Pocket water in small streams.

notes: Maybe the prettiest freshwater fish in North America — emerald with orange and indigo markings. Small but eager.

name: Warmouth (stumpknocker)

tactics.notes: Stumps, lily pads, weedy backwaters.

notes: Sneaky ambush panfish — looks like a small bass with a goggle-eye. Backwater specialist.

name: Spotted sunfish (stumpknocker)

tactics.notes: Cypress knees and overhanging brush.
Tight to tree-root cover.

notes: FL/GA backwater favorite. Small but everywhere — tannin streams and floodplain swamps.

name: Flier

tactics.notes: Slow blackwater swamps and tannin creeks.

notes: Small disc-shaped sunfish; bycatch in southeastern coastal-plain swamps.

name: Chain pickerel

tactics.notes: Weed edges and grass points — fast retrieve. Steel leader.
Strip fast along weed edges.

notes: AKA 'jackfish'. Cold-water aggressor — bite is on when bass shut down. Wire bite tippet recommended.

name: Grass pickerel

tactics.notes: Vegetated backwaters; light tackle.

notes: Smallest pickerel — 10–14 in typical. Usually caught by accident on a panfish bait.

name: Redfin pickerel

tactics.notes: Tannin streams and blackwater swamps; sight-cast along edges.

notes: Tiny southeastern pickerel — vivid red fins. Blackwater specialty species.

name: Brown bullhead

tactics.notes: Soft-bottom holes after dark.

notes: Cousin of yellow bullhead. MI / Great Lakes drainages — solid eating fish in cold months.

name: Black bullhead

tactics.notes: Stagnant, muddy water — they love it.

notes: More tolerant of poor water than channel cats; common in farm-pond stunting populations.

name: White catfish

tactics.notes: Brackish tidal creeks and coastal river mouths.

notes: Brackish-tolerant catfish of southeastern coastal rivers.

name: Smallmouth buffalo

tactics.notes: Bottom-feeders in slow current. Snag-fishing is legal in some TN tailwaters.

notes: Big — 30–50 lb common in TN River reservoirs. Pulls like a truck on light tackle.

name: Bigmouth buffalo

tactics.notes: Hair rig + method ball — same approach as for carp.

notes: Largest sucker species. Some live 100+ years. Often caught by carp anglers.

name: Northern hog sucker

seasons.label: Warm months in clear, gravelly streams

tactics.notes: Dead-drift on the bottom — they're vacuum cleaners.

lures: Garden worm on small hook + split shot
tactics.notes: Same drift, just heavier.

notes: Smallmouth bycatch species. Bizarre forward-mounted mouth for vacuuming gravel.

name: Black redhorse

tactics.notes: Spring spawners stack up below riffles.

notes: Spring sucker run in clear streams. Underrated light-tackle fight.

name: Golden redhorse

tactics.notes: Same as black redhorse — riffle tails on spring runs.

notes: Larger cousin to black redhorse. Spring spawning runs in Midwest + southern rivers.

name: Spotted gar

tactics.notes: Backwaters, oxbows, swamp pockets.

notes: Smaller cousin of longnose gar with distinctive dark spots. FL panhandle and southern oxbows.

name: Florida gar

tactics.notes: Surface-basking in clear FL lakes — sight cast.

notes: FL-endemic; smaller and more colorful than longnose. Everglades, Lake Okeechobee, central FL lakes.

name: Shortnose gar

tactics.notes: TN River chain backwaters and tributary mouths.

notes: Smaller cousin to longnose — short, broad snout. Common in TN/AL/IN river systems.

name: American shad

seasons.label: Spring spawning run on Atlantic-coast rivers

tactics.notes: Tandem rig of two shad darts — bounce through current seams below shoals.
Sinking line, short strips in current.

notes: Spring run on the Roanoke, Cape Fear, Neuse, Savannah, St. Johns. 'Poor man's tarpon' — flashy, jumping fight.

name: Hickory shad

seasons.label: Spring run, slightly ahead of American shad

tactics.notes: Same rivers, often co-mingled with American shad.

notes: Smaller cousin of American shad. Schools heavily on Carolina coastal-plain rivers.

name: American eel

tactics.notes: Set lines overnight in brackish or tidal-river holes.

notes: Catadromous — born in Sargasso Sea, spawns there too. Strong fight; commonly used as striper bait.

name: White perch

tactics.notes: Currituck Sound and tidal tributaries — schoolies stack thick.

notes: Brackish-water resident; smaller cousin of striped bass. NC sound country specialty.

name: Yellow bass

tactics.notes: Mixed schools with whites and hybrids during the run.

notes: Smaller cousin of white bass — bright yellow flanks with dark lateral lines. Excellent panfish on light tackle.

name: Saugeye

tactics.notes: Vertical jig in tailwater eddies and reservoir points.
Contour breaks at dusk.

notes: Sterile sauger × walleye stocked hybrid. Tolerates muddier water than walleye, harder fighting.

name: Tiger trout

tactics.notes: Aggressive predator — strip streamers fast.
Same as brown trout but more aggressive.

notes: Brown × brook sterile hybrid; striking vermiculated pattern. Stocked in a handful of MI lakes.

name: Blacktip shark

lures: Cut ladyfish chunk on circle hook
tactics.notes: Cast or drift live bait at the edge of bait blitzes. 12 in of wire leader minimum.

notes: Acrobatic — bigger blacktips clear the surface entirely. Beach + sound fishing classic.

name: Bull shark

seasons.label: Warm months inshore + far up rivers

tactics.notes: Heavy gear (80+ lb braid). Found in brackish AND fresh rivers — sometimes 100+ mi inland.

notes: Aggressive, fresh-water tolerant. Hard, sustained fight. Common in coastal-plain river systems.

name: Bonnethead shark

tactics.notes: Sight-cast tailing fish on shallow grass flats.
Flats and intracoastal grass beds.

notes: Mini-hammerhead, 2–4 ft typical. Sight-fishing flats target on light tackle — a real treat on fly.

name: Spinner shark

seasons.label: Winter inshore migration off Atlantic coast

tactics.notes: Cast into bait blitzes during the FL East Coast winter migration.

notes: Famous for spiraling clear of the water in pursuit of bait. FL East Coast 'shark blitz' in Jan–Mar is legendary.

name: Lemon shark

lures: Live ladyfish or jack on wire
tactics.notes: Bridges, inlets, and channel edges. Heavy gear.

notes: FL flats + inshore resident. Yellow-hued, deliberate, hard-fighting. Catch-and-release tradition.

name: Great hammerhead

seasons.label: Tarpon-season parallel — they follow the tarpon

lures: Whole live jack on heavy wire
tactics.notes: Bridges + passes following tarpon schools. 130+ lb braid.

notes: Catch & release only (protected). Most often hooked while tarpon fishing in FL Keys + Biscayne passes.

name: Sandbar shark

lures: Cut bait on circle hook + wire
tactics.notes: Beach + pier fishing in 20–50 ft. Bottom rig with shock leader.

notes: 'Brown shark' — workhorse of the surf-shark fishery. Carolinas pier favorite.

name: Nurse shark

lures: Cut bonito or barracuda chunk on bottom
tactics.notes: Anchored over reef ledges, around bridge pilings.

notes: Slow, bulldog fights. Often hooked while bottom-fishing for snapper/grouper. Catch & release.

name: Blacknose shark

lures: Cut shrimp or squid on wire leader
tactics.notes: Surf, sound + nearshore in 10–40 ft.

notes: Small shark (3–5 ft) with distinctive dark snout. Common surf/pier catch.

name: Mutton snapper

tactics.notes: Drop on patch reefs in 30–80 ft. Chum + light leader.

notes: FL Keys + Atlantic reefs. Larger, smarter cousin of mangrove snapper. Excellent table.

name: Lane snapper

tactics.notes: Patch reefs in 15–80 ft. Use small hooks.

notes: Pink + yellow striped snapper. Smaller (1–3 lb), abundant on Atlantic + Gulf patch reefs.

name: Vermilion snapper (beeliner)

tactics.notes: Drop to deep reefs in 120–250 ft. Multi-hook rigs for limits.

notes: Smaller red-snapper cousin in deeper water. Less regulated; year-round target.

name: Cubera snapper

lures: Live blue runner or ladyfish on heavy circle hook
tactics.notes: Wrecks + bridges. 80+ lb braid, heavy fluoro.

notes: Trophy snapper, 30–80 lb common. FL Keys + south FL wrecks; full-moon June/July is the bite.

name: Schoolmaster snapper

tactics.notes: Mangrove roots + bridge pilings in FL Keys.

notes: Yellow-finned small snapper of the Keys. Aggressive on light tackle.

name: Black grouper

lures: Live pinfish on heavy jig head
tactics.notes: Reefs + wrecks in 60–250 ft. Lock the drag.

notes: Largest of the shallow groupers — 60+ lb possible. Verify federal season + size limit.

name: Scamp

tactics.notes: Deep ledges and live-bottom in 100–250 ft.

notes: Considered the best-eating grouper of the Atlantic + Gulf. Smaller (5–15 lb) than gag/black.

name: Snowy grouper

lures: Electric reel + cut squid at 500–900 ft
Whole bonita strip on deep-drop rig
tactics.notes: Bait, weight, push the button.

notes: Deep-drop trophy. Atlantic outer shelf. Catch-and-release for fish over 36 in often required.

name: Warsaw grouper

tactics.notes: 300–800 ft. Heavy current; lots of weight.

notes: 200+ lb possible. Single-fish-per-vessel rule federally — true trophy. Catch & release if you want more.

name: African pompano

lures: Live pinfish on heavy jig head
tactics.notes: Drop on nearshore wrecks in 80–200 ft.

notes: Big-eyed, silver-disced jack. Schools around wrecks; strong sustained runs.

name: Almaco jack

lures: Live blue runner on circle hook
tactics.notes: Drop to wrecks 100–250 ft. Crank fast.

notes: Cousin of amberjack — same wreck habitat, often co-mingled. Excellent table fare (better than AJ).

name: Lookdown

seasons.label: Warm months under dock lights and bridges

tactics.notes: Dock lights at night — perfectly clear water.
Soft mouths — keep tension light.

notes: Bizarre flat-bodied jack — looks like an aluminum coin. Sight-fishing favorite under dock lights.

name: Yellow jack

seasons.label: Warm months on flats and patch reefs

tactics.notes: Cruising flats and channel edges in clear water.
Same areas — light tackle.

notes: Pretty yellow-tailed small jack of the Keys + south FL. Light-tackle bonus species.

name: Banded rudderfish

tactics.notes: Nearshore reefs in 40–120 ft.

notes: Small amberjack cousin — banded pattern. Underrated as table fare; aggressive on light gear.

name: Lesser amberjack

seasons.label: Federal season open in winter when greater AJ closed

tactics.notes: Deep wrecks 150–300 ft.

notes: Distinct from greater AJ — smaller, deeper. Federally regulated separately.

name: Cero mackerel

seasons.label: Year-round in FL Keys + south FL

tactics.notes: Reef edges + nearshore patches. Wire leader recommended.

notes: Keys + south FL cousin of Spanish — larger, with distinctive yellow line down the side. Strong table.

name: Blue marlin

seasons.label: Warm-water Gulf Stream + Gulf of Mexico runs

tactics.notes: High-speed troll 7–9 kts in Gulf Stream. 80+ lb tackle minimum.

notes: Bucket-list pelagic. NC OBX summer + FL Gulf Stream + AL Gulf hold them. Catch & release standard.

name: White marlin

tactics.notes: Slower troll (6 kts) with smaller spread than blue marlin.

notes: Smaller (40–80 lb) than blue marlin but more numerous. OBX summer bite is legendary.

name: Yellowfin tuna

seasons.label: Warm-water months on the Gulf Stream / oil rigs

lures: Skirted ballyhoo on circle hook (6 kts)
tactics.notes: Run-and-gun on bait schools or rip lines. 50–80 lb tackle.

lures: Vertical jig at first light over oil rigs
tactics.notes: Sit over markings; drop and crank.

notes: Premier offshore tuna — fast, hard fight, exceptional sushi. AL/LA oil rigs hold them year-round.

name: Skipjack tuna

tactics.notes: Often mixed with mahi schools.

notes: Smaller tuna (5–15 lb), schooling. Excellent as bonito-style table fish and as live bait for marlin.

name: Atlantic bonito

tactics.notes: Cast through diving birds on bait blitzes.

notes: Spring 'tunny' run on Carolina coast. Hard, fast fight; stronger fishy flavor than albacore.

name: Weakfish (gray trout)

tactics.notes: Sound country and inlets on a moving tide.

notes: NC sound country specialty — population recovering after decades of decline. Soft mouth, hence 'weakfish'.

name: Sand seatrout

tactics.notes: Sandy-bottom grass beds in Gulf bays.

notes: Gulf cousin of spotted sea trout — paler, more silver. Common bycatch in AL/FL bay fishing.

name: Gulf flounder

lures: Live mud minnow on jig head
tactics.notes: Slow drag across sandy bay bottom near oyster bars.

notes: FL/AL Gulf cousin of southern flounder. Three eye-spots on the back is the visual cue.

name: Summer flounder (fluke)

seasons.label: Late spring through fall in NC ocean + sounds

tactics.notes: Drift over sandy bottom 20–80 ft.

notes: NC northern flatfish — distinct from southern flounder. Larger eyes, more teeth, more aggressive.

name: Striped mullet

tactics.notes: Sight-cast — they're herbivores, presentation matters more than pattern.
Cast net is more efficient; rod is for sport.

notes: Largely bait, but the FL fall mullet run (Oct–Nov) is THE event that drives the entire predator chain inshore.

name: White grunt

lures: Cut squid on small circle hook
tactics.notes: Reefs in 30–120 ft — drop straight down with a chum bag.

notes: Schools heavily on Atlantic + Gulf reefs. Fillet-and-fry favorite — known as the 'pig fish' for its grunting sound.

name: Atlantic spadefish

seasons.label: Warm months around nearshore wrecks + buoys

lures: Small piece of cannonball jellyfish on a hook
tactics.notes: Sight-cast schools around nearshore structure.

notes: Beautiful disc-shaped fish — saltwater bluegill. Schools 50+ over buoys and nearshore wrecks.

name: Knobbed porgy

tactics.notes: Drop on live-bottom reefs in 60–150 ft.

notes: Pink-toned reef porgy — common bottom-fishing catch. Excellent table.

name: Tomtate

seasons.label: Year-round in NC + Atlantic surf + reefs

tactics.notes: Pier + surf + jetty fishing on light tackle.

notes: Small grunt — abundant in NC pier and surf. Tasty pan-fried whole.

name: Pinfish

tactics.notes: Grass flats, jetties, dock pilings — easy fish.

notes: Mostly used as live bait for snook, grouper, big trout — but a fun panfish for kids on its own.

name: Golden tilefish

lures: Electric reel + cut squid + bonita strip
Deep-drop multi-hook rig with glow beads
tactics.notes: Mud bottoms 600–900 ft along the continental shelf.

notes: Deep-drop trophy with excellent table fare — sweet, lobster-like. Strict bag limit + slot.

name: Blueline tilefish

lures: Electric reel + cut squid at 200–400 ft
tactics.notes: Mid-shelf hard bottoms.

notes: Shallower cousin of golden tilefish — easier reach, still deep-drop gear. Atlantic ledge species.

name: Swordfish

seasons.label: Year-round in Gulf Stream — daytime + nighttime fisheries

lures: Squid bait on daytime deep-drop rig (1500–1800 ft)
Strip bait under glow stick at night near surface
tactics.notes: Two completely different fisheries: daytime electric-reel deep + nighttime drift in 600 ft.

notes: Premier blue-water target. FL Keys daytime + NC summer night drift. 300+ lb possible.

name: Bigeye tuna

seasons.label: Summer Gulf Stream + offshore canyon edges

tactics.notes: Predawn troll along the canyons. 80+ lb tackle.

notes: Cousin of yellowfin — runs deeper, fights harder. NC canyon edges in summer; FL Gulf Stream year-round.

name: Cisco (lake herring)

tactics.notes: Vertical fish over deep mid-lake basins 30–80 ft. Light line; soft mouths.

notes: Schools heavily over deep cold water. Excellent smoked. MI inland lakes + Great Lakes.

name: Round whitefish

tactics.notes: Ice fish 40–80 ft over hard bottom near Lake Michigan / Superior.

notes: Smaller, paler cousin of lake whitefish. Northern MI ice + early-spring shorelines.

name: Redbreast sunfish

tactics.notes: Sandy / gravel shorelines and slow stream margins.
Sight-fish bedding pairs.

notes: AKA 'robin' — orange-bellied, long pointed ear flap. SE coastal-plain streams and slow rivers.

name: White sucker

lures: Worm on small hook + split shot
tactics.notes: Spring spawners stack up below riffles.

notes: Most-common North American sucker. Spring spawn is the bite — runs by mid-April in MI.

name: Longnose sucker

tactics.notes: Tributary mouths in early spring.

notes: Larger northern sucker. Common spring bycatch on Great Lakes steelhead rivers.

name: Shorthead redhorse

tactics.notes: Riffle tails on spring runs — light tackle.

notes: Brick-red tail, blunt head. Common spring spawn-run sucker — strong light-tackle fight.

name: Quillback

tactics.notes: Slow current and lake shallows; targets the same setups as carp.

notes: Carpsucker with a tall sail-like dorsal. Common bycatch when targeting carp.

name: Gizzard shad

tactics.notes: Primary use is live or cut bait for bass / stripers / blue cats.

notes: The backbone of the forage base in southern reservoirs. Rarely a rod-and-reel target.

name: Threadfin shad

tactics.notes: Live-bait the shad on a balloon for striper / hybrid striper.

notes: Smaller and more cold-sensitive than gizzard shad. Stocked widely in southern reservoirs as striper forage.

name: Alewife

lures: Dip net at night during run
tactics.notes: Mostly netted; rarely a rod target.

notes: Primary forage for Great Lakes salmon. Native population still runs Atlantic-coast rivers.

name: Grass carp

tactics.notes: Stealth approach; cast tight to grazing mouth — they're skittish.
Bait carp gear: heavy line for the long fight.

notes: Asian vegetarian stocked for vegetation control. Sterile (triploid) versions stocked in most states. 30+ lb common.

name: Bighead carp

tactics.notes: Filter-feeder — won't take a hook. Bow + snag rig.

notes: Asian invasive — large heads, big bodies (40+ lb). Ohio + Mississippi + TN River systems.

name: Silver carp

seasons.label: Warm months — jumping at boat noise

tactics.notes: Outboard noise triggers a school-wide leap; dodge or shoot.

notes: The famous 'jumping carp' of the Illinois + Mississippi + TN systems. Filter-feeder; not a rod target.

name: Paddlefish (spoonbill)

seasons.label: Spring snagging season (check state regs each year)

lures: Heavy weighted treble hook on stout rod
tactics.notes: Sweep the rod hard; only legal during state-set snagging seasons.

notes: Primitive plankton-filterer with a paddle-shaped snout. Snagging only — TN, Cumberland, Ohio River systems. Excellent table.

name: Shovelnose sturgeon

tactics.notes: Anchor in moderate current over sand/gravel bars.

notes: Smaller cousin of lake sturgeon — legally kept in IN (verify size + season). Ohio + Wabash rivers.

name: Atlantic sturgeon

seasons.label: Year-round — FEDERALLY ENDANGERED, immediate release required

lures: N/A — protected. Cannot target. If accidentally hooked, release in water without lifting.
tactics.notes: Federally endangered. Catch is a violation if intentionally targeted.

notes: Cape Fear + Roanoke + Altamaha river systems. Bycatch only — federal law requires immediate release.

name: Gulf sturgeon

lures: N/A — protected. Cannot target. If accidentally hooked, release in water immediately.
tactics.notes: Federally threatened. Suwannee + Apalachicola + Mobile systems.

notes: Mostly bycatch. The Suwannee River population is famous for its spectacular leaps in spring.

name: Black buffalo

tactics.notes: Same gear as smallmouth + bigmouth buffalo.

notes: Darker, stockier than smallmouth or bigmouth buffalo. TN River chain + lower Mississippi.

name: Blueback herring

tactics.notes: Concurrent with American + hickory shad runs.

notes: Atlantic river herring — spring-run cousin of American shad. Important bait + small light-tackle target.

name: Black sea bass

tactics.notes: Anchor over reef / wreck in 40–250 ft.

notes: NC + Atlantic mainstay. Aggressive on the drop — limits possible on a good ledge. Big males turn iridescent blue.

name: Sunshine bass

tactics.notes: Schools push shad to surface — run-and-gun.

notes: FL's reciprocal hybrid (striped female × white male — the opposite cross from regular wiper). FL reservoirs stock heavily.

name: Mottled sculpin

tactics.notes: Mostly imitated, not targeted.

notes: Small forage fish of cold streams. Sculpin streamer patterns (Zoo Cougar, Sex Dungeon) are tied in their image to fool big trout.

name: Round goby

tactics.notes: Lake Erie + Lake Michigan rocky shorelines — bycatch when smallmouth fishing.

notes: Invasive Great Lakes nuisance. Eats native fish eggs but is now the major forage that grew the smallmouth average size. Kill rather than release.

name: Sea lamprey

seasons.label: Spring spawning run in Great Lakes tributaries

tactics.notes: Parasitic — kill rather than release. Targeted only by control programs and the occasional curious angler.

notes: Invasive parasite of Great Lakes trout + salmon. Sea Lamprey Control Program traps run in spring.

name: Goldfish (feral)

tactics.notes: Urban ponds + city parks — released aquarium pets that established.

notes: Feral aquarium escapees. Most often caught in city ponds and urban water features. Some grow to 14+ in.

name: Cutthroat trout

seasons.label: Spring through fall in mountain streams + high lakes

tactics.notes: Cutthroat are aggressive but spook easily in clear water — long leaders, light tippet.
Where regs allow — many cutthroat waters are fly-only.

notes: Native trout of the Western US. Multiple subspecies (Yellowstone, Westslope, Bonneville, Snake River fine-spotted, Colorado River, Rio Grande, Greenback) — local angler shorthand is just 'cutts'. Distinctive red/orange slash under the jaw.

name: Bull trout

seasons.label: Open season varies — most water is catch-and-release-only

tactics.notes: Apex char of the Northwest — hit big prey. Where legal, target deep pools and confluences.

notes: Federally threatened in much of its range. Catch-and-release only on most waters; some MT / ID flows have very limited harvest seasons. Verify regs before keeping any. Large, white-spotted on dark olive body.

name: Mountain whitefish

seasons.label: Year-round; fattest in winter when trout slow down

tactics.notes: Deep dead-drifts with weight. They sit on the bottom in slower runs.
Winter is whitefish season — when the trout are sluggish, the whities keep eating.

notes: Native to most Western trout rivers. Often dismissed as 'trash fish' but actually a great winter target — strong fight on light tackle, smoke beautifully.

name: Arctic grayling

seasons.label: Open water in mountain streams + glacial lakes

lures: Small attractor dries — grayling rise eagerly
tactics.notes: Sail-finned upper Missouri / Madison drainage relicts and high-alpine lakes.

notes: Iconic spotted dorsal that flares like a sail. Big Hole, Madison headwaters, and high-elevation MT lakes are the strongholds — declining elsewhere.

name: Kokanee salmon

tactics.notes: Troll 1.0-1.4 mph at 40-80 ft from spring into summer; deeper as the thermocline drops.
Snag schools on a fish finder; drop and vertically jig.

notes: Landlocked sockeye. Major Western reservoir target — Flathead, Pend Oreille, Granby, Strawberry, Tahoe. Their flesh is the deepest orange in freshwater; excellent smoked.

name: Golden trout

tactics.notes: Backpacker fish — most populations require a hike to 10,000+ ft.

notes: CA native, transplanted to alpine lakes across the Rocky Mountain West. Vivid orange-and-gold flanks with parr marks. A true high-country quarry.

name: White sturgeon

seasons.label: Year-round catch-and-release in ID Snake River

lures: Whole sucker on heavy circle hook
tactics.notes: Heavy gear (150+ lb braid). Anchor in deep holes — Hells Canyon, C.J. Strike pool.

notes: Pacific cousin of the lake sturgeon; can exceed 10 ft and 500 lb. ID Snake River population is the most accessible — strict catch-and-release, barbless hooks, no removing from the water.
